name: ING
mission: The entire experience of planning a series of seminars with ING was inspirational. They did an amazing job. The ING team welcomed input along the way which allowed each session to be even better than the one before it. We will continue to work with ING as I am confident that they will continue to provide value in our work.

dimension_explanations.alignment: With an alignment score of 24/50, the charity serves a religious and cultural cause that may have lower urgency for donors focused on immediate survival needs [3]. While it offers opportunities for sadaqah, its specialized focus on education and training results in a lower fit for general Muslim donor priorities [7][8].
dimension_explanations.impact: The organization focuses on capacity building through interfaith panels and youth training, reporting high satisfaction ratings from educators [4][5]. However, the program expense ratio is 39.5%, and the impact score of 16/50 reflects the difficulty in measuring long-term social change compared to direct-aid models [1][2].

baseline_narrative.summary: Islamic Networks Inc, also known as Islamic Networks Group (ING), is a non-profit organization dedicated to promoting a nuanced understanding of Muslims and other faith-based or racial communities [3][5]. The organization reported a total revenue of $688,262 and operates programs such as the Islamic Speakers Bureau and Inclusion and Belonging Training to counter prejudice [1][2].

areas_for_improvement.detail: The current <cite id="10">program expense ratio of 39.5%</cite> is well below the <cite id="11">industry standard of 75%</cite>, suggesting high administrative costs relative to direct output.

case_against.summary: The primary concern is the <cite id="10">39.5% program expense ratio</cite>, which indicates that less than half of every dollar directly funds programs.

confidence.notes: Financial data is solid, but impact evidence is self-reported.

alignment.explanation: The charity serves a <cite id="24">niche religious-cultural cause</cite> that is often underserved, though it lacks <cite id="25">zakat-eligibility</cite> which limits its fit for some Muslim donors.
alignment.improvement: The organization could improve alignment by <cite id="26">clearly stating its sadaqah-only status</cite> on a dedicated giving page.

credibility.explanation: Donors can find confidence in ING's <cite id="1">33-year operating history</cite> and its <cite id="21">independent board composition of 66%</cite>. However, credibility is limited by the <cite id="20">unverified status of its long-term outcomes</cite>.
credibility.improvement: To strengthen credibility, the organization should <cite id="20">seek external evaluations</cite> and publish more recent audited financials.

impact.explanation: Impact is driven by a <cite id="22">capacity-building model</cite> that reached <cite id="15">400,000 people live</cite> in a single year. However, the <cite id="10">39.5% program ratio</cite> is a significant drag on the impact score.
impact.improvement: ING could improve by <cite id="23">publishing consistent beneficiary counts</cite> to allow for cost-per-beneficiary analysis.

strengths.detail: The organization spends <cite id="9">$0.00 to raise every $1</cite> in contributions, a rare benchmark that maximizes the utility of donor acquisitions.
ING maintains a <cite id="8">96% satisfaction rate among educators</cite> and has successfully <cite id="13">trained 200 Muslim teens</cite> for community leadership.
With <cite id="12">5.2 months of working capital</cite> and <cite id="14">net assets of $267,556</cite>, the organization demonstrates resilient financial health for its size.

strengths_deep_dive: Islamic Networks Inc excels in educational reach and stakeholder engagement. Their 2020 data shows <cite id="15">400,000 live audience attendees</cite> and an <cite id="16">educator effectiveness rating of 89%</cite>. Beyond live events, their digital footprint is substantial, with <cite id="17">870,000 views of recorded talks</cite>. This reach is supported by a <cite id="18">3-year revenue CAGR of 6.0%</cite>, showing steady growth in their ability to fund these outreach programs.
rich_narrative.summary: Founded in <cite id="1">1993</cite>, Islamic Networks Inc (ING) is a specialized educational non-profit dedicated to <cite id="2">fostering peace and cross-cultural understanding</cite> through outreach and interfaith engagement. The organization operates as a capacity-building entity, providing <cite id="3">professional development, interfaith panels, and online curriculum resources</cite> to counter prejudice. In 2023, the organization reported <cite id="4">total revenue of $622,869</cite>, with a significant portion of its funding derived from <cite id="5">$563,428 in direct contributions</cite>. Despite its modest financial scale, ING maintains a broad reach, reporting a <cite id="6">projected annual reach of 2 million people</cite> and <cite id="7">50,000 monthly website visitors</cite> seeking educational materials. Their model focuses on systemic change by training educators and community leaders, boasting a <cite id="8">96% educator satisfaction rate</cite>. Financially, the organization is notable for its <cite id="9">fundraising efficiency of $0.00 per $1 raised</cite>, ensuring that donor outreach costs do not deplete incoming gifts. However, the organization allocates a <cite id="10">program expense ratio of 39.5%</cite>, which is significantly lower than the <cite id="11">peer median of 89.1%</cite> for similar religious and cultural organizations. This lean program spending is balanced by a strong liquidity position, as the charity holds <cite id="12">5.2 months of working capital</cite>, providing a buffer for its specialized educational initiatives.
